question:
If $p(x)= a_0 + a_1 x + \cdots + a_m x^m$ is a polynomial with real
coefficients $a_i$, then set
\[
\Gamma(p(x)) = a_0^2 + a_1^2 + \cdots + a_m^2.
\]
Let $F(x) = 3x^2+7x+2$. Find, with proof, a polynomial $g(x)$ with
real coefficients such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $g(0)=1$, and
\item[(ii)] $\Gamma(f(x)^n) = \Gamma(g(x)^n)$
\end{enumerate}
for every integer $n \geq 1$.
solution:
Solution. For any polynomial \( p(x) \), let \( \gamma(p(x))=p(x) p\left(x^{-1}\right) \), which is a Laurent polynomial (an expression of the form \( \sum_{j=m}^{n} a_{j} x^{j} \) where \( a_{j} \) are constants and \( m, n \) are integers, not necessarily nonnegative). Then \( \Gamma(p(x)) \) equals the coefficient of \( x^{0} \) in \( \gamma(p(x)) \).

We have \( f(x)=(3 x+1)(x+2) \). Since
\[
\gamma(x+2)=(x+2)\left(x^{-1}+2\right)=\left(1+2 x^{-1}\right)(1+2 x)=\gamma(1+2 x),
\]
and \( \gamma(p(x) q(x))=\gamma(p(x)) \gamma(q(x)) \) for any polynomials \( p(x) \) and \( q(x) \), we find
\[
\gamma\left(f(x)^{n}\right)=\gamma\left((3 x+1)^{n}\right) \gamma\left((x+2)^{n}\right)=\gamma\left((3 x+1)^{n}\right) \gamma\left((1+2 x)^{n}\right)=\gamma\left(g(x)^{n}\right),
\]
where \( g(x)=(3 x+1)(1+2 x)=6 x^{2}+5 x+1 \). Taking coefficients of \( x^{0} \), we obtain \( \Gamma\left(f(x)^{n}\right)=\Gamma\left(g(x)^{n}\right) \). Moreover \( g(0)=1 \), so we are done.

Remark. One could also show by brute force that \( \Gamma\left(\left(\sum a_{i} x^{i}\right)\left(\sum b_{j} x^{j}\right)\right) \) is unchanged by reversing the order of the \( b_{j} \), without mentioning \( \gamma \) or Laurent polynomials. The coefficient of \( x^{0} \) in a Laurent polynomial can also be expressed as a contour integral, via the residue theorem; hence one could begin a solution with the observation
\[
\Gamma(p(x))=\frac{1}{2 \pi i} \oint_{|z|=1} p(z) p\left(z^{-1}\right) \frac{d z}{z}
\]

Remark. The solution is not unique: for any integer \( k \geq 1 \), the polynomials \( g(x)=\left(3 x^{k}+1\right)\left(2 x^{k}+1\right) \) and \( g(x)=\left(3 x^{k}-1\right)\left(2 x^{k}-1\right) \) also have the desired properties. We do not know if these are the only ones. Using the fact that the ring \( \mathbb{R}[x, 1 / x] \) of Laurent polynomials is a unique factorization domain (UFD), we could prove that these are the only ones if we could prove the following conjecture of Greg Kuperberg (communicated electronically):

Suppose \( h_{1}, h_{2} \in \mathbb{R}[x, 1 / x] \) satisfy \( h_{1}(x)=h_{1}(1 / x) \) and \( h_{2}(x)=h_{2}(1 / x) \).
Then the following are equivalent:
(a) The coefficient of \( x^{0} \) in \( h_{1}(x)^{n} \) equals the coefficient of \( x^{0} \) of \( h_{2}(x)^{n} \) for all positive integers \( n \).
(b) There exists \( j \in \mathbb{R}[x, 1 / x] \) such that for \( i=1,2 \) we have \( h_{i}(x)=j\left(\epsilon_{i} x^{k_{i}}\right) \) for some \( \epsilon_{i} \in\{1,-1\} \) and \( k_{i} \geq 1 \).
(It is easy to prove that (b) implies (a).)

descriptive_long.question:
If $polyfun(variablex)= coeffzero + coeffone\,variablex + \cdots + coeffem\,variablex^{degreemax}$ is a polynomial with real coefficients $coeffvar$, then set
\[
sumsquare(polyfun(variablex)) = coeffzero^{2} + coeffone^{2} + \cdots + coeffem^{2}.
\]
Let $targetpoly(variablex) = 3\,variablex^{2}+7\,variablex+2$. Find, with proof, a polynomial $resultpoly(variablex)$ with
real coefficients such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $resultpoly(0)=1$, and
\item[(ii)] $sumsquare(givenpoly(variablex)^{exponentcounter}) = sumsquare(resultpoly(variablex)^{exponentcounter})$
\end{enumerate}
for every integer $exponentcounter \ge 1$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. For any polynomial \( polyfun(variablex) \), let \( laurop(polyfun(variablex)) = polyfun(variablex)\, polyfun\left(variablex^{-1}\right) \), which is a Laurent polynomial (an expression of the form \( \sum_{integervar=degreemax}^{exponentcounter} coeffvar_{integervar}\,variablex^{integervar} \) where the \( coeffvar_{integervar} \) are constants and \( degreemax, exponentcounter \) are integers, not necessarily non-negative). Then \( sumsquare(polyfun(variablex)) \) equals the coefficient of \( variablex^{0} \) in \( laurop(polyfun(variablex)) \).

We have \( givenpoly(variablex) = (3\,variablex+1)(variablex+2) \). Since
\[
laurop(variablex+2) = (variablex+2)\bigl(variablex^{-1}+2\bigr)=\bigl(1+2\,variablex^{-1}\bigr)\bigl(1+2\,variablex\bigr)=laurop(1+2\,variablex),
\]
and \( laurop(polyfun(variablex)\,q(variablex)) = laurop(polyfun(variablex))\,laurop(q(variablex)) \) for any polynomials \( polyfun(variablex) \) and \( q(variablex) \), we obtain
\[
laurop\bigl(givenpoly(variablex)^{exponentcounter}\bigr)=laurop\bigl((3\,variablex+1)^{exponentcounter}\bigr)\,laurop\bigl((variablex+2)^{exponentcounter}\bigr)=laurop\bigl((3\,variablex+1)^{exponentcounter}\bigr)\,laurop\bigl((1+2\,variablex)^{exponentcounter}\bigr)=laurop\bigl(resultpoly(variablex)^{exponentcounter}\bigr),
\]
where \( resultpoly(variablex) = (3\,variablex+1)(1+2\,variablex)=6\,variablex^{2}+5\,variablex+1 \). Taking coefficients of \( variablex^{0} \) gives \( sumsquare\bigl(givenpoly(variablex)^{exponentcounter}\bigr)=sumsquare\bigl(resultpoly(variablex)^{exponentcounter}\bigr) \). Moreover, \( resultpoly(0)=1 \), so the required polynomial has been found.

Remark. One can also check directly that
\( sumsquare\bigl((\sum coeffvar_{integervar} variablex^{integervar})(\sum b_{integervar} variablex^{integervar})\bigr) \)
is unchanged when the \( b_{integervar} \) are taken in the reverse order, without mentioning \( laurop \) or Laurent polynomials. The coefficient of \( variablex^{0} \) in a Laurent polynomial can also be written as a contour integral, via the residue theorem; thus one may begin with
\[
sumsquare(polyfun(variablex))=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{|complexvar|=1} polyfun(complexvar)\,polyfun\bigl(complexvar^{-1}\bigr)\,\frac{d complexvar}{complexvar}.
\]

Remark. The solution is not unique: for any integer \( indexfactor \ge 1 \) the polynomials
\( resultpoly(variablex)=\bigl(3\,variablex^{indexfactor}+1\bigr)\bigl(2\,variablex^{indexfactor}+1\bigr) \) and
\( resultpoly(variablex)=\bigl(3\,variablex^{indexfactor}-1\bigr)\bigl(2\,variablex^{indexfactor}-1\bigr) \)
also satisfy the stated properties. We do not know if these are the only examples. Using that the ring \( \mathbb{R}[variablex,1/variablex] \) of Laurent polynomials is a unique-factorization domain, we could prove they are the only ones if we could establish the following conjecture of Greg Kuperberg:

Suppose \( laurone, laurtwo \in \mathbb{R}[variablex,1/variablex] \) satisfy \( laurone(variablex)=laurone(1/variablex) \) and \( laurtwo(variablex)=laurtwo(1/variablex) \). Then the following are equivalent:
(a) the coefficient of \( variablex^{0} \) in \( laurone(variablex)^{exponentcounter} \) equals that in \( laurtwo(variablex)^{exponentcounter} \) for every positive integer \( exponentcounter \);
(b) there exists \( integervar \in \mathbb{R}[variablex,1/variablex] \) such that for \( i=1,2 \) we have
\( h_{i}(variablex)=integervar\bigl(signchoice_{i}\,variablex^{indexset}\bigr) \) for some \( signchoice_{i}\in\{1,-1\} \) and \( indexset \ge 1 \).
(It is straightforward that (b) implies (a).)

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
If $watermelon(sunflower)= goldfinch + woodpecker sunflower + \cdots + hummingbird sunflower^{cheeseball}$ is a polynomial with real
coefficients $nightingale$, then set
\[
pineapple(watermelon(sunflower)) = goldfinch^2 + woodpecker^2 + \cdots + hummingbird^2.
\]
Let $dragonfly(sunflower) = 3sunflower^2+7sunflower+2$. Find, with proof, a polynomial $marshmallow(sunflower)$ with
real coefficients such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $marshmallow(0)=1$, and
\item[(ii)] $pineapple(windmill(sunflower)^{boardwalk}) = pineapple(marshmallow(sunflower)^{boardwalk})$
\end{enumerate}
for every integer $boardwalk \geq 1$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. For any polynomial \( watermelon(sunflower) \), let \( caterpillar(watermelon(sunflower))=watermelon(sunflower) watermelon\left(sunflower^{-1}\right) \), which is a Laurent polynomial (an expression of the form \( \sum_{peppercorn=cheeseball}^{boardwalk} a_{\peppercorn} sunflower^{\peppercorn} \) where \( a_{\peppercorn} \) are constants and \( cheeseball, boardwalk \) are integers, not necessarily nonnegative). Then \( pineapple(watermelon(sunflower)) \) equals the coefficient of \( sunflower^{0} \) in \( caterpillar(watermelon(sunflower)) \).

We have \( windmill(sunflower)=(3 sunflower+1)(sunflower+2) \). Since
\[
caterpillar(sunflower+2)=(sunflower+2)\left(sunflower^{-1}+2\right)=\left(1+2 sunflower^{-1}\right)(1+2 sunflower)=caterpillar(1+2 sunflower),
\]
and \( caterpillar(watermelon(sunflower) q(sunflower))=caterpillar(watermelon(sunflower)) caterpillar(q(sunflower)) \) for any polynomials \( watermelon(sunflower) \) and \( q(sunflower) \), we find
\[
caterpillar\left(windmill(sunflower)^{boardwalk}\right)=caterpillar\left((3 sunflower+1)^{boardwalk}\right) caterpillar\left((sunflower+2)^{boardwalk}\right)=caterpillar\left((3 sunflower+1)^{boardwalk}\right) caterpillar\left((1+2 sunflower)^{boardwalk}\right)=caterpillar\left(marshmallow(sunflower)^{boardwalk}\right),
\]
where \( marshmallow(sunflower)=(3 sunflower+1)(1+2 sunflower)=6 sunflower^{2}+5 sunflower+1 \). Taking coefficients of \( sunflower^{0} \), we obtain \( pineapple\left(windmill(sunflower)^{boardwalk}\right)=pineapple\left(marshmallow(sunflower)^{boardwalk}\right) \). Moreover \( marshmallow(0)=1 \), so we are done.

Remark. One could also show by brute force that \( pineapple\left(\left(\sum nightingale sunflower^{peppercorn}\right)\left(\sum b_{\peppercorn} sunflower^{peppercorn}\right)\right) \) is unchanged by reversing the order of the \( b_{\peppercorn} \), without mentioning \( caterpillar \) or Laurent polynomials. The coefficient of \( sunflower^{0} \) in a Laurent polynomial can also be expressed as a contour integral, via the residue theorem; hence one could begin a solution with the observation
\[
pineapple(watermelon(sunflower))=\frac{1}{2 \pi i} \oint_{|butterscotch|=1} watermelon(butterscotch) watermelon\left(butterscotch^{-1}\right) \frac{d butterscotch}{butterscotch}
\]

Remark. The solution is not unique: for any integer \( lamborghini \geq 1 \), the polynomials \( marshmallow(sunflower)=\left(3 sunflower^{lamborghini}+1\right)\left(2 sunflower^{lamborghini}+1\right) \) and \( marshmallow(sunflower)=\left(3 sunflower^{lamborghini}-1\right)\left(2 sunflower^{lamborghini}-1\right) \) also have the desired properties. We do not know if these are the only ones. Using the fact that the ring \( \mathbb{R}[sunflower, 1 / sunflower] \) of Laurent polynomials is a unique factorization domain (UFD), we could prove that these are the only ones if we could prove the following conjecture of Greg Kuperberg (communicated electronically):

Suppose \( tortoise, armadillo \in \mathbb{R}[sunflower, 1 / sunflower] \) satisfy \( tortoise(sunflower)=tortoise(1 / sunflower) \) and \( armadillo(sunflower)=armadillo(1 / sunflower) \).
Then the following are equivalent:
(a) The coefficient of \( sunflower^{0} \) in \( tortoise(sunflower)^{boardwalk} \) equals the coefficient of \( sunflower^{0} \) of \( armadillo(sunflower)^{boardwalk} \) for all positive integers \( boardwalk \).
(b) There exists \( peppercorn \in \mathbb{R}[sunflower, 1 / sunflower] \) such that for \( i=1,2 \) we have \( h_{i}(sunflower)=peppercorn\left(blueberry sunflower^{orangutan}\right) \) for some \( blueberry \in\{1,-1\} \) and \( orangutan \geq 1 \).
(It is easy to prove that (b) implies (a).)

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
If $flatfunc(stillpoint)= flowingzero + flowingone stillpoint + \cdots + flowingmax stillpoint^{baseline}$ is a polynomial with real
coefficients $flowingiota$, then set
\[
\oppositum(flatfunc(stillpoint)) = flowingzero^2 + flowingone^2 + \cdots + flowingmax^2.
\]
Let $varipoly(stillpoint) = 3stillpoint^2+7stillpoint+2$. Find, with proof, a polynomial $fixedpoly(stillpoint)$ with
real coefficients such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $fixedpoly(0)=1$, and
\item[(ii)] $\oppositum(constpoly(stillpoint)^{fractional}) = \oppositum(fixedpoly(stillpoint)^{fractional})$
\end{enumerate}
for every integer $fractional \geq 1$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. For any polynomial \( flatfunc(stillpoint) \), let \( \stability(flatfunc(stillpoint))=flatfunc(stillpoint) flatfunc\left(stillpoint^{-1}\right) \), which is a Laurent polynomial (an expression of the form \( \sum_{macrofunc=baseline}^{fractional} a_{macrofunc} stillpoint^{macrofunc} \) where \( a_{macrofunc} \) are constants and \( baseline, fractional \) are integers, not necessarily nonnegative). Then \( \oppositum(flatfunc(stillpoint)) \) equals the coefficient of \( stillpoint^{0} \) in \( \stability(flatfunc(stillpoint)) \).

We have \( constpoly(stillpoint)=(3 stillpoint+1)(stillpoint+2) \). Since
\[
\stability(stillpoint+2)=(stillpoint+2)\left(stillpoint^{-1}+2\right)=\left(1+2 stillpoint^{-1}\right)(1+2 stillpoint)=\stability(1+2 stillpoint),
\]
and \( \stability(flatfunc(stillpoint) q(stillpoint))=\stability(flatfunc(stillpoint)) \stability(q(stillpoint)) \) for any polynomials \( flatfunc(stillpoint) \) and \( q(stillpoint) \), we find
\[
\stability\left(constpoly(stillpoint)^{fractional}\right)=\stability\left((3 stillpoint+1)^{fractional}\right) \stability\left((stillpoint+2)^{fractional}\right)=\stability\left((3 stillpoint+1)^{fractional}\right) \stability\left((1+2 stillpoint)^{fractional}\right)=\stability\left(fixedpoly(stillpoint)^{fractional}\right),
\]
where \( fixedpoly(stillpoint)=(3 stillpoint+1)(1+2 stillpoint)=6 stillpoint^{2}+5 stillpoint+1 \). Taking coefficients of \( stillpoint^{0} \), we obtain \( \oppositum\left(constpoly(stillpoint)^{fractional}\right)=\oppositum\left(fixedpoly(stillpoint)^{fractional}\right) \). Moreover \( fixedpoly(0)=1 \), so we are done.

Remark. One could also show by brute force that \( \oppositum\left(\left(\sum flowingiota stillpoint^{i}\right)\left(\sum b_{macrofunc} stillpoint^{macrofunc}\right)\right) \) is unchanged by reversing the order of the \( b_{macrofunc} \), without mentioning \( \stability \) or Laurent polynomials. The coefficient of \( stillpoint^{0} \) in a Laurent polynomial can also be expressed as a contour integral, via the residue theorem; hence one could begin a solution with the observation
\[
\oppositum(flatfunc(stillpoint))=\frac{1}{2 \pi i} \oint_{|realness|=1} flatfunc(realness) flatfunc\left(realness^{-1}\right) \frac{d realness}{realness}
\]

Remark. The solution is not unique: for any integer \( continuity \geq 1 \), the polynomials \( fixedpoly(stillpoint)=\left(3 stillpoint^{continuity}+1\right)\left(2 stillpoint^{continuity}+1\right) \) and \( fixedpoly(stillpoint)=\left(3 stillpoint^{continuity}-1\right)\left(2 stillpoint^{continuity}-1\right) \) also have the desired properties. We do not know if these are the only ones. Using the fact that the ring \( \mathbb{R}[stillpoint, 1 / stillpoint] \) of Laurent polynomials is a unique factorization domain (UFD), we could prove that these are the only ones if we could prove the following conjecture of Greg Kuperberg (communicated electronically):

Suppose \( shortone, shorttwo \in \mathbb{R}[stillpoint, 1 / stillpoint] \) satisfy \( shortone(stillpoint)=shortone(1 / stillpoint) \) and \( shorttwo(stillpoint)=shorttwo(1 / stillpoint) \).
Then the following are equivalent:
(a) The coefficient of \( stillpoint^{0} \) in \( shortone(stillpoint)^{fractional} \) equals the coefficient of \( stillpoint^{0} \) of \( shorttwo(stillpoint)^{fractional} \) for all positive integers \( fractional \).
(b) There exists \( macrofunc \in \mathbb{R}[stillpoint, 1 / stillpoint] \) such that for \( i=1,2 \) we have \( h_{i}(stillpoint)=macrofunc\left(\heaviness_{i} stillpoint^{continuum}\right) \) for some \( \heaviness_{i} \in\{1,-1\} \) and \( continuum \geq 1 \).
(It is easy to prove that (b) implies (a).)

garbled_string.question:
If $rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp)= lkjhgfdsa + poiuytrew qzxwvtnp + \cdots + mnbvcxzas qzxwvtnp^{wsxedcrfv}$ is a polynomial with real
coefficients $qazwsxedc_i$, then set
\[
pasdfghjk(rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp)) = lkjhgfdsa^2 + poiuytrew^2 + \cdots + mnbvcxzas^2.
\]
Let $yhnujmiko(qzxwvtnp) = 3qzxwvtnp^2+7qzxwvtnp+2$. Find, with proof, a polynomial $olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp)$ with
real coefficients such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $olpknjmbv(0)=1$, and
\item[(ii)] $pasdfghjk(plokmijnb(qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla}) = pasdfghjk(olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla})$
\end{enumerate}
for every integer $hjgrksla \geq 1$.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. For any polynomial \( rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp) \), let \( lpoiuytre(rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp))=rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp) \, rfvtgbyhn\left(qzxwvtnp^{-1}\right) \), which is a Laurent polynomial (an expression of the form \( \sum_{uytresdfg=wsxedcrfv}^{hjgrksla} lkjhgfdsa_{uytresdfg} qzxwvtnp^{uytresdfg} \) where \( lkjhgfdsa_{uytresdfg} \) are constants and \( wsxedcrfv, hjgrksla \) are integers, not necessarily non-negative). Then \( pasdfghjk(rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp)) \) equals the coefficient of \( qzxwvtnp^{0} \) in \( lpoiuytre(rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp)) \).

We have \( plokmijnb(qzxwvtnp)=(3 qzxwvtnp+1)(qzxwvtnp+2) \). Since
\[
lpoiuytre(qzxwvtnp+2)=(qzxwvtnp+2)\left(qzxwvtnp^{-1}+2\right)=\left(1+2 qzxwvtnp^{-1}\right)(1+2 qzxwvtnp)=lpoiuytre(1+2 qzxwvtnp),
\]
and \( lpoiuytre(rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp)\, olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp))=lpoiuytre(rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp))\, lpoiuytre(olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp)) \) for any polynomials \( rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp) \) and \( olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp) \), we find
\[
lpoiuytre\left(plokmijnb(qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla}\right)=lpoiuytre\left((3 qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla}\right)\, lpoiuytre\left((qzxwvtnp+2)^{hjgrksla}\right)=lpoiuytre\left((3 qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla}\right)\, lpoiuytre\left((1+2 qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla}\right)=lpoiuytre\left(olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla}\right),
\]
where \( olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp)=(3 qzxwvtnp+1)(1+2 qzxwvtnp)=6 qzxwvtnp^{2}+5 qzxwvtnp+1 \). Taking coefficients of \( qzxwvtnp^{0} \), we obtain \( pasdfghjk\left(plokmijnb(qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla}\right)=pasdfghjk\left(olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla}\right) \). Moreover \( olpknjmbv(0)=1 \), so we are done.

Remark. One could also show by brute force that \( pasdfghjk\left(\left(\sum qazwsxedc_{uytresdfg} qzxwvtnp^{uytresdfg}\right)\left(\sum poiuytrew_{uytresdfg} qzxwvtnp^{uytresdfg}\right)\right) \) is unchanged by reversing the order of the \( poiuytrew_{uytresdfg} \), without mentioning \( lpoiuytre \) or Laurent polynomials. The coefficient of \( qzxwvtnp^{0} \) in a Laurent polynomial can also be expressed as a contour integral, via the residue theorem; hence one could begin a solution with the observation
\[
pasdfghjk(rfvtgbyhn(qzxwvtnp))=\frac{1}{2 \pi i} \oint_{|asdfghjk|=1} rfvtgbyhn(asdfghjk)\, rfvtgbyhn\left(asdfghjk^{-1}\right) \frac{d asdfghjk}{asdfghjk}
\]

Remark. The solution is not unique: for any integer \( vbnmrewq \geq 1 \), the polynomials \( olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp)=\left(3 qzxwvtnp^{vbnmrewq}+1\right)\left(2 qzxwvtnp^{vbnmrewq}+1\right) \) and \( olpknjmbv(qzxwvtnp)=\left(3 qzxwvtnp^{vbnmrewq}-1\right)\left(2 qzxwvtnp^{vbnmrewq}-1\right) \) also have the desired properties. We do not know if these are the only ones. Using the fact that the ring \( \mathbb{R}[qzxwvtnp, 1 / qzxwvtnp] \) of Laurent polynomials is a unique factorization domain (UFD), we could prove that these are the only ones if we could prove the following conjecture of Greg Kuperberg (communicated electronically):

Suppose \( xcvbnmlkj(qzxwvtnp), zxcvbnmas(qzxwvtnp) \in \mathbb{R}[qzxwvtnp, 1 / qzxwvtnp] \) satisfy \( xcvbnmlkj(qzxwvtnp)=xcvbnmlkj(1 / qzxwvtnp) \) and \( zxcvbnmas(qzxwvtnp)=zxcvbnmas(1 / qzxwvtnp) \).
Then the following are equivalent:
(a) The coefficient of \( qzxwvtnp^{0} \) in \( xcvbnmlkj(qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla} \) equals the coefficient of \( qzxwvtnp^{0} \) of \( zxcvbnmas(qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla} \) for all positive integers \( hjgrksla \).
(b) There exists \( uytresdfg \in \mathbb{R}[qzxwvtnp, 1 / qzxwvtnp] \) such that for \( i=1,2 \) we have \( xcvbnmlkj_{i}(qzxwvtnp)=uytresdfg\left(mkiolpbnv_{i} qzxwvtnp^{qwertyuio_{i}}\right) \) for some \( mkiolpbnv_{i} \in\{1,-1\} \) and \( qwertyuio_{i} \geq 1 \).
(It is easy to prove that (b) implies (a).)

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
\Gamma\!\bigl(p\bigr)\;=\!
\sum_{i,j,k\ge 0}a_{i,j,k}^{2},\qquad 
\Bigl(p(x,y,z)=\!\sum_{i,j,k\ge 0}a_{i,j,k}\,x^{i}y^{j}z^{k}\Bigr)
\]  
denote the squared Euclidean norm of the (finite) real coefficient array of a
three-variable polynomial.  
Fix  
\[
F(x,y,z)=\bigl(2x+1\bigr)\bigl(6y+1\bigr)\bigl(15z+1\bigr).
\]

A.  Prove that there exists a real polynomial $g(x,y,z)$ such that  
(i) $g(0,0,0)=1$;  
(ii) $\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)=\Gamma\!\bigl(g^{\,n}\bigr)$ holds for every
integer $n\ge 1$;  
(iii) $g\not\equiv F$.

B.  Let  
\[
h(x,y,z)=\bigl(\alpha_{1}x+1\bigr)\bigl(\alpha_{2}y+1\bigr)
         \bigl(\alpha_{3}z+1\bigr),\qquad \alpha_{j}\in\mathbb{R},
\]
be any product of three univariate linear factors whose constant terms equal
$1$.  Determine precisely those triples
$(\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2},\alpha_{3})$ that satisfy  
\[
\Gamma\!\bigl(h^{\,n}\bigr)=\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)\quad\forall\,n\ge 1,
\]
and prove that every solution is obtained from $(2,6,15)$ by an arbitrary
permutation of the entries together with independent sign changes.

C.  Let $H(x,y,z)$ be \emph{any} real polynomial of finite total degree such
that  
\[
H(0,0,0)=1,\qquad 
\Gamma\!\bigl(H^{\,n}\bigr)=\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)\quad\forall\,n\ge 1.
\]
Show that $H$ must be of the form singled out in~(B):  
there exist a permutation $\sigma\in\mathfrak{S}_{3}$ and signs
$\varepsilon_{j}\in\{\!+1,-1\!\}$ for which  
\[
H(x,y,z)=
\bigl(\varepsilon_{1}2\,x+1\bigr)\,
\bigl(\varepsilon_{2}6\,y+1\bigr)\,
\bigl(\varepsilon_{3}15\,z+1\bigr)
\]
after the variables $(x,y,z)$ have been reordered by~$\sigma$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
\Theta_{p}(\boldsymbol{\theta})=
p\!\bigl(e^{\mathrm{i}\theta_{1}},
         e^{\mathrm{i}\theta_{2}},
         e^{\mathrm{i}\theta_{3}}\bigr),\qquad
\boldsymbol{\theta}=(\theta_{1},\theta_{2},\theta_{3})\in\mathbb{R}^{3},
\]
and denote by  
\[
d\mu=\frac{d\theta_{1}\,d\theta_{2}\,d\theta_{3}}{(2\pi)^{3}}
\]
the normalised Haar measure on the torus $T^{3}=[0,2\pi]^{3}$.
Orthogonality of the exponentials gives Parseval's identity  
\[
\Gamma\!\bigl(p^{\,n}\bigr)=
\int_{T^{3}}\!\bigl|\Theta_{p}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr|^{2n}\,d\mu
      \qquad(n\ge 0).
\tag{$\mathcal{P}$}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
A.  A non-trivial example
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Permuting $y$ and $z$ in $F$ produces  
\[
g(x,y,z)=\bigl(2x+1\bigr)\bigl(15y+1\bigr)\bigl(6z+1\bigr).
\]
Clearly $g(0,0,0)=1$ and $g\not\equiv F$.  A permutation of the
indeterminates merely re-labels the coefficient array, hence preserves the
Euclidean norm; therefore $\Gamma\!\bigl(g^{\,n}\bigr)=\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)$
for every $n\ge 1$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.  Classification inside the linear family
--------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.1  The singularity sets that must coincide
--------------------------------------------------------------------

For a one-variable linear form $L_{a}(t)=1+at$ put  
\[
S_{n}(a)=\Gamma\!\bigl(L_{a}(t)^{\,n}\bigr)=
        \sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k}^{2}a^{\,2k},
\qquad 
G_{a}(z)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}S_{n}(a)z^{n}
       =\frac{1}{\sqrt{\bigl(1-(1+a)^{2}z\bigr)
                       \bigl(1-(1-a)^{2}z\bigr)}} .
\tag{1}
\]

Because the three variables are independent,  
\[
\sum_{n\ge 0}\Gamma\!\bigl(h^{\,n}\bigr)z^{n}
     =\prod_{j=1}^{3}G_{\alpha_{j}}(z),
\qquad
\sum_{n\ge 0}\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)z^{n}
     =G_{2}(z)G_{6}(z)G_{15}(z).
\]
Hence
\[
\prod_{j=1}^{3}G_{\alpha_{j}}(z)=G_{2}(z)G_{6}(z)G_{15}(z).
\tag{2}
\]

Each $G_{a}$ is a square-root of a rational function whose only
singularities (branch points) are at  
\[
z=\frac{1}{(1+a)^{2}},\qquad z=\frac{1}{(1-a)^{2}}.
\]
Equality~(2) therefore forces the two multisets of branch points to be
identical:
\[
\bigl\{\tfrac{1}{(1+\alpha_{1})^{2}},\tfrac{1}{(1-\alpha_{1})^{2}},
       \tfrac{1}{(1+\alpha_{2})^{2}},\tfrac{1}{(1-\alpha_{2})^{2}},
       \tfrac{1}{(1+\alpha_{3})^{2}},\tfrac{1}{(1-\alpha_{3})^{2}}\bigr\}
      =\Bigl\{\tfrac{1}{9},1,\tfrac{1}{25},\tfrac{1}{49},
              \tfrac{1}{196},\tfrac{1}{256}\Bigr\}.
\tag{3}
\]
(The right-hand set comes from $a\in\{2,6,15\}$.)

Taking square roots and reciprocals transforms~(3) into the much simpler
statement
\[
\bigl\{\lvert1+\alpha_{1}\rvert,\lvert1-\alpha_{1}\rvert,
       \lvert1+\alpha_{2}\rvert,\lvert1-\alpha_{2}\rvert,
       \lvert1+\alpha_{3}\rvert,\lvert1-\alpha_{3}\rvert\bigr\}
      =\{1,3,5,7,14,16\}.
\tag{4}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.2  Reconstructing $\lvert\alpha_{j}\rvert$ from the six integers
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Fix any real $\beta\neq\pm1$.  The unordered pair
\[
\bigl\lvert1+\beta\bigr\rvert,\;\bigl\lvert1-\beta\bigr\rvert
\]
always consists of two \emph{distinct} positive numbers whose difference
is exactly $2$.  Conversely, if $u>v>0$ and $u-v=2$, then  
\[
\{\!u,v\!\}=\{\lvert1+\beta\rvert,\lvert1-\beta\rvert\}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\beta=\tfrac{u-v}{2}\cdot\tfrac{u+v}{2}.
\tag{5}
\]
Hence each $\beta$ corresponds to a \emph{pair} of positive numbers that
differ by $2$, and vice versa.

Among the six positive integers in the right-hand side of~(4) there are
exactly four pairs whose members differ by $2$,
\[
(1,3),\;(3,5),\;(5,7),\;(14,16).
\]
Because we must partition the multiset $\{1,3,5,7,14,16\}$ into \emph{three}
disjoint pairs each differing by $2$, a simple ``smallest-element'' argument
shows that there is a unique perfect matching:
\[
\{1,3\}\cup\{5,7\}\cup\{14,16\}.
\]
Indeed, the smallest element $1$ can only be paired with $3$;
removing $\{1,3\}$ leaves $\{5,7,14,16\}$, whose smallest element $5$ forces
the pair $\{5,7\}$; the remaining two numbers automatically form the third
pair.

Applying the reconstruction formula~(5) to the three pairs yields
\[
\lvert\alpha_{1}\rvert=2,\qquad
\lvert\alpha_{2}\rvert=6,\qquad
\lvert\alpha_{3}\rvert=15,
\tag{6}
\]
up to ordering.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.3  Sign independence and variable permutations
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Because $S_{n}(-a)=S_{n}(a)$ for every $n$ and every real $a$
(cf.\ the definition of $S_{n}$ above), replacing any $\alpha_{j}$ by
$-\alpha_{j}$ leaves $\Gamma\!\bigl(h^{\,n}\bigr)$ unchanged.
Furthermore, permuting the variables $(x,y,z)$ only re-orders the
coefficients, hence also preserves their Euclidean norm.
Combining these two observations with~(6) gives the full set of solutions:
\[
(\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2},\alpha_{3})
=\bigl(\varepsilon_{1}2,\varepsilon_{2}6,\varepsilon_{3}15\bigr),
\quad\varepsilon_{j}\in\{\!+1,-1\!\},
\]
up to an arbitrary permutation of the three entries.  This completes the
classification required in~B.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.  Global rigidity
--------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.1  From equality of moments to pointwise equality
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Set  
\[
U(\boldsymbol{\theta})=\bigl|\Theta_{H}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr|^{2},
\qquad 
V(\boldsymbol{\theta})=\bigl|\Theta_{F}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr|^{2}
     \quad(\boldsymbol{\theta}\in T^{3}).
\]
Both $U$ and $V$ are continuous, non-negative functions on the compact
$T^{3}$.  By assumption  
\[
\int_{T^{3}}U(\boldsymbol{\theta})^{\,n}\,d\mu
     =\Gamma\!\bigl(H^{\,n}\bigr)
     =\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)
     =\int_{T^{3}}V(\boldsymbol{\theta})^{\,n}\,d\mu
     \qquad\forall\,n\ge 0.
\tag{7}
\]

\emph{Lemma.}
If two bounded measurable functions $U,V\ge 0$ satisfy
$\int U^{n}\,d\mu=\int V^{n}\,d\mu$ for every $n$, then $U=V$ almost
everywhere.

\emph{Proof of the Lemma.}
Put $W=U-V$.  If $W\not\equiv 0$, the set
$E_{+}=\{\boldsymbol{\theta}:W(\boldsymbol{\theta})>0\}$
has positive measure.
Choose $\varepsilon>0$ such that 
$E_{\varepsilon}=\{\boldsymbol{\theta}:W(\boldsymbol{\theta})\ge \varepsilon\}$
still has positive measure.
On $E_{\varepsilon}$ we have $U\ge V+\varepsilon$, hence
$U^{n}\ge V^{n}+n\varepsilon V^{\,n-1}$.
Integrating and using (7) with $n$ large gives a contradiction.
$\hfill\square$

Applying the Lemma we obtain $U=V$ almost everywhere, hence everywhere
because the difference $U-V$ is a trigonometric polynomial and therefore
real-analytic on $T^{3}$.  Consequently
\[
H(x,y,z)\,H\!\bigl(x^{-1},y^{-1},z^{-1}\bigr)=
F(x,y,z)\,F\!\bigl(x^{-1},y^{-1},z^{-1}\bigr)
\quad\text{as Laurent polynomials.}
\tag{8}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.2  Unique factorisation of Laurent polynomials
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Put  
\[
R=\mathbb{R}[X^{\pm1},Y^{\pm1},Z^{\pm1}],\qquad
\star:\,R\longrightarrow R,\;
\bigl(f^{\star}\bigr)(X,Y,Z)=f(X^{-1},Y^{-1},Z^{-1}).
\]
The ring $R$ is a unique factorisation domain (localisation of the UFD
$\mathbb{R}[X,Y,Z]$), and $\star$ is an involutive automorphism.
Equation~(8) can be rewritten as  
\[
H\,H^{\star}=F\,F^{\star}\qquad\text{in }R.
\tag{9}
\]

Factorisation of the right-hand side is explicit:
\[
F\,F^{\star}=
\bigl(1+2X\bigr)\bigl(1+2X^{-1}\bigr)\,
\bigl(1+6Y\bigr)\bigl(1+6Y^{-1}\bigr)\,
\bigl(1+15Z\bigr)\bigl(1+15Z^{-1}\bigr).
\tag{10}
\]
Each linear factor is irreducible in $R$, and any two distinct factors are
coprime.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.3  Locating the factors that divide $H$
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Write  
\[
H=\sum_{i,j,k\ge 0}c_{i,j,k}X^{i}Y^{j}Z^{k},\qquad c_{0,0,0}=1.
\tag{11}
\]
Thus $H$ is a genuine polynomial (no negative exponents).  
Suppose, for instance, that the factor $1+2X^{-1}$ from~(10) divided $H$:
then $H=(1+2X^{-1})Q$ with $Q\in R$.  
Expanding, the lowest $X$-exponent occurring in $H$ would be \emph{negative}
(unless $Q$ contained a compensating $X$ factor, in which case the constant
term of $H$ would change from $1$).  Either alternative contradicts (11).
Hence $1+2X^{-1}$ cannot divide $H$, and the same argument applies to
$1+6Y^{-1}$ and $1+15Z^{-1}$.

Therefore, for each variable, exactly one of the two factors in the
corresponding pair in~(10) divides $H$, and the multiplicity must be~$1$
because it is~$1$ in~(10).  Thus
\[
H=\varepsilon\,
(1+\varepsilon_{1}2X)\,(1+\varepsilon_{2}6Y)\,(1+\varepsilon_{3}15Z),
\tag{12}
\]
where $\varepsilon,\varepsilon_{1},\varepsilon_{2},\varepsilon_{3}
      \in\{\!+1,-1\!\}$.
The constant term condition $c_{0,0,0}=1$ forces $\varepsilon=1$.
Finally, permuting $(X,Y,Z)$ corresponds to permuting $(x,y,z)$,
so~(12) translates back to the claimed form of $H$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.4  Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Combining~(12) with the independent sign choices
$\varepsilon_{j}\in\{\!+1,-1\!\}$ and an arbitrary permutation
$\sigma\in\mathfrak{S}_{3}$ we arrive precisely at the family singled
out in~B, completing the proof of~C.

\hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension & more variables:  We moved from univariate to
  trivariate polynomials, turning a one-dimensional coefficient array
  into a three-dimensional one.

• Additional constraints:  Besides finding one suitable g, the solver
  must now classify all polynomials h fulfilling the quadratic-norm
  equalities, not merely exhibit one example.

• Deeper theory:  The solution demands familiarity with
  Laurent-polynomial rings in several variables, their unique
  factorisation, and the behaviour of quadratic norms under variable
  inversion.  These topics do not appear in the original problem.

• Interacting concepts:  The argument blends algebraic factorisation,
  symmetries of Euclidean norms, invariance under coefficient
  permutation, and multiplicativity of the γ-map.

• More steps & insight:  Establishing γ for three variables, proving its
  multiplicativity, analysing constant terms, and executing the UFD
  uniqueness argument collectively require significantly more work and
  conceptual depth than the original single-variable manipulation.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
\Gamma\!\bigl(p\bigr)\;=\!
\sum_{i,j,k\ge 0}a_{i,j,k}^{2},\qquad 
\Bigl(p(x,y,z)=\!\sum_{i,j,k\ge 0}a_{i,j,k}\,x^{i}y^{j}z^{k}\Bigr)
\]  
denote the squared Euclidean norm of the (finite) real coefficient array of a
three-variable polynomial.  
Fix  
\[
F(x,y,z)=\bigl(2x+1\bigr)\bigl(6y+1\bigr)\bigl(15z+1\bigr).
\]

A.  Prove that there exists a real polynomial $g(x,y,z)$ such that  
(i) $g(0,0,0)=1$;  
(ii) $\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)=\Gamma\!\bigl(g^{\,n}\bigr)$ holds for every
integer $n\ge 1$;  
(iii) $g\not\equiv F$.

B.  Let  
\[
h(x,y,z)=\bigl(\alpha_{1}x+1\bigr)\bigl(\alpha_{2}y+1\bigr)
         \bigl(\alpha_{3}z+1\bigr),\qquad \alpha_{j}\in\mathbb{R},
\]
be any product of three univariate linear factors whose constant terms equal
$1$.  Determine precisely those triples
$(\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2},\alpha_{3})$ that satisfy  
\[
\Gamma\!\bigl(h^{\,n}\bigr)=\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)\quad\forall\,n\ge 1,
\]
and prove that every solution is obtained from $(2,6,15)$ by an arbitrary
permutation of the entries together with independent sign changes.

C.  Let $H(x,y,z)$ be \emph{any} real polynomial of finite total degree such
that  
\[
H(0,0,0)=1,\qquad 
\Gamma\!\bigl(H^{\,n}\bigr)=\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)\quad\forall\,n\ge 1.
\]
Show that $H$ must be of the form singled out in~(B):  
there exist a permutation $\sigma\in\mathfrak{S}_{3}$ and signs
$\varepsilon_{j}\in\{\!+1,-1\!\}$ for which  
\[
H(x,y,z)=
\bigl(\varepsilon_{1}2\,x+1\bigr)\,
\bigl(\varepsilon_{2}6\,y+1\bigr)\,
\bigl(\varepsilon_{3}15\,z+1\bigr)
\]
after the variables $(x,y,z)$ have been reordered by~$\sigma$.

Throughout write  
\[
\Theta_{p}(\boldsymbol{\theta})=
p\!\bigl(e^{\mathrm{i}\theta_{1}},
         e^{\mathrm{i}\theta_{2}},
         e^{\mathrm{i}\theta_{3}}\bigr),\qquad
\boldsymbol{\theta}=(\theta_{1},\theta_{2},\theta_{3})\in\mathbb{R}^{3},
\]
and denote by  
\[
d\mu=\frac{d\theta_{1}\,d\theta_{2}\,d\theta_{3}}{(2\pi)^{3}}
\]
the normalised Haar measure on the torus $T^{3}=[0,2\pi]^{3}$.
Orthogonality of the exponentials gives Parseval's identity  
\[
\Gamma\!\bigl(p^{\,n}\bigr)=
\int_{T^{3}}\!\bigl|\Theta_{p}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr|^{2n}\,d\mu
      \qquad(n\ge 0).
\tag{$\mathcal{P}$}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
A.  A non-trivial example
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Permuting $y$ and $z$ in $F$ produces  
\[
g(x,y,z)=\bigl(2x+1\bigr)\bigl(15y+1\bigr)\bigl(6z+1\bigr).
\]

indeterminates merely re-labels the coefficient array, hence preserves the

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.  Classification inside the linear family
--------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.1  The singularity sets that must coincide
--------------------------------------------------------------------

For a one-variable linear form $L_{a}(t)=1+at$ put  
\[
S_{n}(a)=\Gamma\!\bigl(L_{a}(t)^{\,n}\bigr)=

\qquad

                       \bigl(1-(1-a)^{2}z\bigr)}} .
\tag{1}
\]

\[
\sum_{n\ge 0}\Gamma\!\bigl(h^{\,n}\bigr)z^{n}
     =\prod_{j=1}^{3}G_{\alpha_{j}}(z),
\qquad
\sum_{n\ge 0}\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)z^{n}
     =G_{2}(z)G_{6}(z)G_{15}(z).

\[
\prod_{j=1}^{3}G_{\alpha_{j}}(z)=G_{2}(z)G_{6}(z)G_{15}(z).
\tag{2}

Each $G_{a}$ is a square-root of a rational function whose only
singularities (branch points) are at  
\[
z=\frac{1}{(1+a)^{2}},\qquad z=\frac{1}{(1-a)^{2}}.
\]

identical:
\[
\bigl\{\tfrac{1}{(1+\alpha_{1})^{2}},\tfrac{1}{(1-\alpha_{1})^{2}},
       \tfrac{1}{(1+\alpha_{2})^{2}},\tfrac{1}{(1-\alpha_{2})^{2}},
       \tfrac{1}{(1+\alpha_{3})^{2}},\tfrac{1}{(1-\alpha_{3})^{2}}\bigr\}
      =\Bigl\{\tfrac{1}{9},1,\tfrac{1}{25},\tfrac{1}{49},
              \tfrac{1}{196},\tfrac{1}{256}\Bigr\}.
\tag{3}

\[
\bigl\{\lvert1+\alpha_{1}\rvert,\lvert1-\alpha_{1}\rvert,

       \lvert1+\alpha_{3}\rvert,\lvert1-\alpha_{3}\rvert\bigr\}
      =\{1,3,5,7,14,16\}.
\tag{4}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Fix any real $\beta\neq\pm1$.  The unordered pair
\[
\bigl\lvert1+\beta\bigr\rvert,\;\bigl\lvert1-\beta\bigr\rvert
\]
always consists of two \emph{distinct} positive numbers whose difference
is exactly $2$.  Conversely, if $u>v>0$ and $u-v=2$, then  
\[
\{\!u,v\!\}=\{\lvert1+\beta\rvert,\lvert1-\beta\rvert\}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\beta=\tfrac{u-v}{2}\cdot\tfrac{u+v}{2}.
\tag{5}
\]

differ by $2$, and vice versa.

\[

\]

\[

\]

Applying the reconstruction formula~(5) to the three pairs yields
\[
\lvert\alpha_{1}\rvert=2,\qquad
\lvert\alpha_{2}\rvert=6,\qquad
\lvert\alpha_{3}\rvert=15,
\tag{6}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.3  Sign independence and variable permutations
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Because $S_{n}(-a)=S_{n}(a)$ for every $n$ and every real $a$

Furthermore, permuting the variables $(x,y,z)$ only re-orders the

Combining these two observations with~(6) gives the full set of solutions:
\[

=\bigl(\varepsilon_{1}2,\varepsilon_{2}6,\varepsilon_{3}15\bigr),
\quad\varepsilon_{j}\in\{\!+1,-1\!\},
\]

classification required in~B.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.  Global rigidity
--------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.1  From equality of moments to pointwise equality
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Set  
\[
U(\boldsymbol{\theta})=\bigl|\Theta_{H}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr|^{2},
\qquad 
V(\boldsymbol{\theta})=\bigl|\Theta_{F}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr|^{2}
     \quad(\boldsymbol{\theta}\in T^{3}).
\]
Both $U$ and $V$ are continuous, non-negative functions on the compact
$T^{3}$.  By assumption  
\[
\int_{T^{3}}U(\boldsymbol{\theta})^{\,n}\,d\mu
     =\Gamma\!\bigl(H^{\,n}\bigr)
     =\Gamma\!\bigl(F^{\,n}\bigr)
     =\int_{T^{3}}V(\boldsymbol{\theta})^{\,n}\,d\mu
     \qquad\forall\,n\ge 0.
\tag{7}
\]

\emph{Lemma.}
If two bounded measurable functions $U,V\ge 0$ satisfy
$\int U^{n}\,d\mu=\int V^{n}\,d\mu$ for every $n$, then $U=V$ almost
everywhere.

\emph{Proof of the Lemma.}
Put $W=U-V$.  If $W\not\equiv 0$, the set
$E_{+}=\{\boldsymbol{\theta}:W(\boldsymbol{\theta})>0\}$
has positive measure.
Choose $\varepsilon>0$ such that 
$E_{\varepsilon}=\{\boldsymbol{\theta}:W(\boldsymbol{\theta})\ge \varepsilon\}$
still has positive measure.
On $E_{\varepsilon}$ we have $U\ge V+\varepsilon$, hence

Integrating and using (7) with $n$ large gives a contradiction.
$\hfill\square$

Applying the Lemma we obtain $U=V$ almost everywhere, hence everywhere
because the difference $U-V$ is a trigonometric polynomial and therefore

\[
H(x,y,z)\,H\!\bigl(x^{-1},y^{-1},z^{-1}\bigr)=
F(x,y,z)\,F\!\bigl(x^{-1},y^{-1},z^{-1}\bigr)
\quad\text{as Laurent polynomials.}
\tag{8}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.2  Unique factorisation of Laurent polynomials
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Put  
\[
R=\mathbb{R}[X^{\pm1},Y^{\pm1},Z^{\pm1}],\qquad
\star:\,R\longrightarrow R,\;
\bigl(f^{\star}\bigr)(X,Y,Z)=f(X^{-1},Y^{-1},Z^{-1}).
\]
The ring $R$ is a unique factorisation domain (localisation of the UFD
$\mathbb{R}[X,Y,Z]$), and $\star$ is an involutive automorphism.
Equation~(8) can be rewritten as  
\[
H\,H^{\star}=F\,F^{\star}\qquad\text{in }R.
\tag{9}
\]

Factorisation of the right-hand side is explicit:
\[
F\,F^{\star}=
\bigl(1+2X\bigr)\bigl(1+2X^{-1}\bigr)\,
\bigl(1+6Y\bigr)\bigl(1+6Y^{-1}\bigr)\,
\bigl(1+15Z\bigr)\bigl(1+15Z^{-1}\bigr).
\tag{10}
\]
Each linear factor is irreducible in $R$, and any two distinct factors are
coprime.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.3  Locating the factors that divide $H$
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Write  
\[
H=\sum_{i,j,k\ge 0}c_{i,j,k}X^{i}Y^{j}Z^{k},\qquad c_{0,0,0}=1.
\tag{11}
\]

Therefore, for each variable, exactly one of the two factors in the
corresponding pair in~(10) divides $H$, and the multiplicity must be~$1$
because it is~$1$ in~(10).  Thus
\[
H=\varepsilon\,
(1+\varepsilon_{1}2X)\,(1+\varepsilon_{2}6Y)\,(1+\varepsilon_{3}15Z),
\tag{12}
\]
where $\varepsilon,\varepsilon_{1},\varepsilon_{2},\varepsilon_{3}
      \in\{\!+1,-1\!\}$.
The constant term condition $c_{0,0,0}=1$ forces $\varepsilon=1$.
Finally, permuting $(X,Y,Z)$ corresponds to permuting $(x,y,z)$,

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.4  Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Combining~(12) with the independent sign choices
$\varepsilon_{j}\in\{\!+1,-1\!\}$ and an arbitrary permutation
$\sigma\in\mathfrak{S}_{3}$ we arrive precisely at the family singled
out in~B, completing the proof of~C.

\hfill$\square$
